post.content:
Given billions of text messages and a list of keywords. design the index for the messages in order to efficiently retrieve messages that contain given keywords. Suppose each message can be identified and retrieved by a unique sequence number.

Task 1: Design data structure to organize the keyword index.
Task 2: How to search messages that contain a single keyword.
Task 3: How to search messages that contain multiple keywords. Optional coding: implement the method.

My solution - 
Let's form a trie with given keywords. Then pick up each message one by one and search it's words in trie. This way form a map of [Keyword , {messageId1, messageId2,...}]
But, interviewer said number of messages are in billions so we can't store these many messageIds in a map. 


Can anyone think of a better solution?

Looks like a search engine problem. remove stop words, stemming, build inverted index(offline map reduce jobs), then rank and return based on index search by keyword. \nMap<word,Document obj(doc id,freq/rank)> we can have only top 1000 docs for ex in a PQ, based of rank/freq.\nFor multiple keywords, need to see if there is a intersection of the docs from the output, the more number of keywords in the doc,  top in the outcome.

First, you need to get rid of the trie. If you are going to build a map <keyword, [messagesIds]> you can directly insert the keywords in the map. Since you are processing billions of text messages you cannot fit that map in the memory of a single machine. So I would replace your map with a distributed Key-Value store.
I think HDFS + Spark Job would be the best way to do this
Check several implementations in the document  - https://citeseerx.ist.psu.edu/viewdoc/download?doi=10.1.1.121.2728&rep=rep1&type=pdf. Lucene is the engine Elasticsearch uses.
If comfortable, Can you please suggest what location/team was this asked at and how did it go?
I think inverted index should be the approach, similar to how elastic search works.
